summary: Spolsky criticizes Windows Vista's shutdown menu for offering fifteen different ways to turn off a laptop, arguing that excessive choice creates unhappiness rather than satisfaction. Drawing on Barry Schwartz's 'The Paradox of Choice,' he demonstrates how most shutdown options could be merged or eliminated. He walks through a systematic reduction from nine menu options down to a single 'b'bye' button that handles locking, sleeping, hibernating, and powering off intelligently. The post concludes that both Microsoft and the open source movement share a flawed design philosophy of equating more options with better software.
key_points: Windows Vista presents users with up to 15 different ways to shut down a laptop, creating unnecessary cognitive burden every time they leave their computer
Most shutdown options (sleep, hibernate, lock, switch user, log off, restart) can be logically merged into a single intelligent 'away' mode that handles transitions automatically
The paradox of choice applies directly to software design: more options don't make users happier, they make users more anxious and confused
Both Microsoft and open source share a consensus-driven design philosophy that conflates 'making everybody happy' with offering every possible option
Each individual option seems defensible in isolation, but the aggregate effect is a confusing experience for non-technical users
money_quotes: Every time you want to leave your computer, you have to choose between nine, count them, nine options: two icons and seven menu items.
The more choices you give people, the harder it is for them to choose, and the unhappier they'll feel.
iPods don't even have an on/off switch.
If you've spoken to a non-geek recently, you may have noticed that they have no idea what the difference is between 'sleep' and 'hibernate.'
Inevitably, you are going to think of a long list of intelligent, defensible reasons why each of these options is absolutely, positively essential. Don't bother. I know.
Each additional choice makes complete sense until you find yourself explaining to your uncle that he has to choose between 15 different ways to turn off a laptop.

This highlights a style of software design shared by Microsoft and the open source movement, in both cases driven by a desire for consensus and for 'Making Everybody Happy,' but it's based on the misconceived notion that lots of choices make people happy, which we really need to rethink.
key_insight: Good software design means ruthlessly reducing choices rather than exposing every possible option, because each individually defensible option contributes to an collectively overwhelming experience.
